stage-intentionally.label: Stage Intentionally
stage-intentionally.summary: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.title: Stage One File, Not Everything
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` and `styles.css` both changed, but the next snapshot is only about navigation. Stage `index.html` and list the staged names.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.hint: Choose only the paths that belong to this checkpoint.

help.concept: Path-specific staging selects one coherent change while leaving unrelated working-tree edits outside the next commit.
help.hint_1: Do not broaden the staging boundary just to make status quieter.
help.hint_2: Review the staged paths or patch before moving on.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..

printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>

printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..

try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.title: Inspect the Exact Staged Patch
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` is already staged while `styles.css` remains unstaged. Inspect the staged patch and then compare it with short status.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.hint: Inspect the exact version currently selected for the next commit.

help.concept: git diff --staged previews what the next commit would record, not every working-tree edit.
help.hint_1: A staged patch is different from the unstaged working-tree patch.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the selected content matches the task before recording it.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.title: Add a Second Related File
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The staged navigation change also needs `nav.js`; `styles.css` is still unrelated. Stage `nav.js` and verify that exactly two files are staged.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.hint: Choose only the paths that belong to this checkpoint.

help.concept: A focused snapshot may contain several files when they serve the same change.
help.hint_1: Do not broaden the staging boundary just to make status quieter.
help.hint_2: Review the staged paths or patch before moving on.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html

' > index.html

cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js

git -C community-site add -- index.html

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.title: Verify the Boundary Before Commit
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The navigation files are staged and the palette edit is not. Use staged and unstaged name-only views to prove the boundary.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: Checking both sides of the index confirms what will be committed and what will remain for later.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

single-choice-1.title: Staging Area Purpose
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To prepare changes for a commit

multi-choice-1.title: Purpose of Staging
multi-choice-1.prompt: What is the purpose of staging in Git? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.a: To prepare specific changes for a commit

multi-choice-2.title: Inspecting Staged Changes
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which command allows you to inspect staged changes in Git?
multi-choice-2.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

drag-reorder-1.title: Staging Workflow Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps in the correct order for staging and committing a file in Git.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

fill-blank-1.title: Command to Stage a File
fill-blank-1.prompt: Fill in the blank to stage a file named `index.html`.
fill-blank-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-2.title: Unstaging a File
fill-blank-2.prompt: Fill in the blank to unstage a file named `index.html`.
fill-blank-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Build the next snapshot one purpose at a time, review the staged version, and reverse a staging decision without losing work.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

stage-intentionally-sketch0.title: Build the Next Snapshot
stage-intentionally-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Staging is how you choose the contents of the next commit. When several files changed, avoid treating “everything” as the default selection. Start with the path that serves the current purpose.

The staging area receives the file’s current version, while the working file remains editable. This gives you a stable proposal to review without freezing the rest of the project.

Selective staging is the bridge between messy real work and clear history.

stage-intentionally-sketch1.title: Review the Proposed Snapshot
stage-intentionally-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A staged diff compares the staging area with the latest commit. It answers the question: **what would this commit record if I created it now?**

That is different from a plain diff, which shows working-tree edits that are not yet staged. Use both views when a file may have changed again after staging.

A final staged review catches missing paths, accidental additions, and stale versions before history changes.

stage-intentionally-sketch2.title: Change the Selection, Not the File
stage-intentionally-sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A staging decision is reversible. Unstaging a path removes it from the proposed snapshot while leaving the working copy intact.

Afterward, short status should show the change on the working-tree side rather than the staging side. The work still exists; only its membership in the next commit changed.

This is the right correction when an edit is valuable but belongs to a later checkpoint.

stage-intentionally-sketch3.title: Keep One Purpose Together
stage-intentionally-sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A strong commit is not necessarily tiny. It is coherent. A new navigation link and the style that makes it usable may belong together, while an unrelated event correction should wait.

Status, unstaged diff, and staged diff are three views of the same editorial decision. Use them to make the boundary visible before writing the message.

When another maintainer reads the commit later, its contents and message should tell the same story.
